title: scenario_in_custody_death
description: A person dies while in police custody. Tests immediate response obligations, scene preservation, mandatory independent oversight notification, and officer conduct during a critical incident investigation under Canadian law.

timeline.title: Arrest and Transport
timeline.description: An intoxicated male is arrested for causing a disturbance and transported to cells.

timeline.title: Cell Check
timeline.description: Thirty minutes after booking, an officer conducts a cell check and finds the prisoner unresponsive.

timeline.title: Emergency Response
timeline.description: Officer calls for help. CPR is initiated. EMS is summoned.

timeline.title: Death Pronounced
timeline.description: EMS works for 30 minutes. The prisoner is pronounced deceased at scene.

timeline.title: Duty Inspector Notified
timeline.description: Duty Inspector arrives and begins assessing mandatory notification obligations.

timeline.title: Independent Oversight Assumes Jurisdiction
timeline.description: The independent oversight body is notified and assumes jurisdiction of the investigation.

node-1.title: Unresponsive Prisoner in Cell
node-1.situation: You are conducting a routine cell check 30 minutes after booking an intoxicated male who was arrested for causing a disturbance. The subject was checked at booking — he was conscious, responsive, and placed in a recovery position. On your current check, you find him face-up, unresponsive, not breathing visibly. His skin is pale.
node-1.question: What is your immediate response?

options.label: A
options.text: Act immediately: (1) Shout for immediate assistance and radio 'Medical emergency — cells — prisoner unresponsive'; (2) Unlock and open the cell — do not delay access while waiting for a second officer unless the scene is actively unsafe; (3) Begin primary assessment — check for pulse and breathing; (4) Begin CPR immediately if no pulse — continue until EMS arrives and assumes care; (5) Have dispatch notify EMS as Code 3 (emergency); (6) Do not move or relocate the prisoner from the cell unless required for CPR space — cell scene preservation will be important; (7) Advise your supervisor immediately.

options.label: B
options.text: Wait for a second officer before entering the cell — policy requires two officers for cell access at all times.

options.label: C
options.text: Assess the prisoner through the cell door — enter only if you determine he is definitely unresponsive to avoid contaminating the scene.

node-2.title: Death Pronounced — Mandatory Notification Obligations
node-2.situation: EMS worked for over 30 minutes but was unable to resuscitate the prisoner. He has been pronounced deceased at scene. EMS has preserved the cell. The duty inspector is asking what the mandatory notification obligations are and who controls the scene.
node-2.question: What are the mandatory notifications and who has jurisdiction over the scene?

options.label: A
options.text: An in-custody death triggers mandatory notifications: (1) Independent oversight body — in Ontario the SIU (or provincial equivalent: ASIRT in Alberta, IIOS in BC, SIRT in Nova Scotia) must be notified immediately. An in-custody death is a 'serious incident' under provincial police oversight legislation. The independent investigator assumes jurisdiction over the scene and the investigation; (2) The Office of the Chief Coroner must be notified — in-custody deaths are mandatory coroner's cases and require a coroner's inquest in most jurisdictions; (3) No scene alteration — the cell and all contents must be preserved exactly. Nothing is to be removed or moved until the independent investigator clears it; (4) Segregate involved officers — officers who had custody of, transported, or monitored the prisoner should be separated pending independent statement collection.

options.label: B
options.text: The police service conducts its own internal investigation first — call the SIU only if the internal investigation reveals policy violations.

options.label: C
options.text: Clean the cell and document the current state with photographs — preserving dignity for the deceased is the immediate priority.

node-3.title: Involved Officer's Conduct During the Investigation
node-3.situation: You are the officer who conducted the last cell check before the prisoner died. You are advised by your duty inspector that you are a 'witness officer' in the SIU investigation. You are told not to discuss the incident with colleagues. Another officer who was on the block at the same time approaches you and says 'We should compare notes before the SIU gets here.'
node-3.question: How do you respond to your colleague's suggestion to compare notes?

options.label: A
options.text: Decline clearly and firmly: 'I've been told not to discuss the incident and we need to keep our accounts independent.' Comparing notes before independent investigators conduct their interviews is improper — it risks tainting witness accounts and can constitute obstruction of an independent investigation. Advise your supervisor immediately that the colleague made this suggestion, so the supervisor can address the issue with both officers and ensure proper segregation. Your role as a witness officer is to cooperate fully and provide an accurate, independent account to the SIU investigator when asked. Do not destroy, alter, or withhold any records related to your interactions with the prisoner.

options.label: B
options.text: Agree to briefly compare notes — you want to make sure there are no inconsistencies in the timeline that could look bad for the service.

options.label: C
options.text: Say nothing to anyone and refuse to cooperate with the SIU investigation pending union advice.

node-4.title: Scenario Complete
node-4.situation: The SIU investigation and coroner's inquest found that the prisoner died of acute intoxication — a medical event not caused by any officer action. Cell check intervals were identified as insufficient for high-risk intoxicated prisoners. The inquest recommended 15-minute checks for intoxicated prisoners (down from 30 minutes). All officers were found to have acted appropriately during the emergency response and subsequent investigation. The officer who was approached by the colleague reported it immediately — this was noted as professional conduct consistent with investigative integrity. In-custody deaths require immediate, transparent, and legally precise response — from life-saving efforts through to independent oversight cooperation.
node-4.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
